title: everything in one platform
description: Artwork Archive review; user cataloguing 20+ years of work
reveals: Buyers frame the win as consolidation+accuracy across decades, not features. AA already owns all-in-one inventory, so Artelier must differentiate on what AA lacks: deterministic pricing and the sale workflow.
quote: everything in one platform and keep an accurate inventory

title: affirmed my sense of myself as a professional artist
description: Artwork Archive review; identity-first adoption
reveals: The product sells identity before utility. Lead onboarding and pricing copy with professional legitimacy; frame the engine as 'price like a pro,' not 'calculate faster.'
quote: affirmed my sense of myself as a professional artist

title: the gallerist wished every artist were as organized
description: Artwork Archive review; external gatekeeper validation
reveals: Validation artists crave is external, from gatekeepers. Design the One Sheet to impress the person on the other side of the table, not just to store data.
quote: the gallerist wished every artist they worked with were as organized

title: more information about how to handle consignments
description: Artwork Archive review; consignment workflow gap
reveals: Even satisfied incumbent users hit a wall on consignment/exhibitions, the exact multi-party money logic Artelier's Settlement waterfall handles. A namable wedge.
quote: would like more information about how to handle consignments

title: distinguish historical artists from current contacts
description: Artwork Archive review; roster/contact modeling weakness
reveals: Roster vs contact separation is a known incumbent weakness. Artelier's per-artist seat model makes this structural rather than a tagging hack, which matters for the manager ICP.
quote: not an easy way to distinguish historical artists from current contacts

title: spreadsheets quickly become cumbersome
description: Independent review of inventory tooling; the spreadsheet failure mode
reveals: The true competitor is the spreadsheet; its failure mode is manual updating plus labeling plus certificates. Target the breaking moment in top-of-funnel copy.
quote: spreadsheets quickly becomes cumbersome and requires quite a bit of manual updating

title: my spreadsheet just doesnt update itself
description: Etsy seller forum; raw overwhelm
reveals: Raw emotional language: exhaustion, drowning, wants something simple. Empathy-first hook will beat feature copy at top of funnel.
quote: My spreadsheet just doesn't update itself

title: many do not own their data they offload to the gallery
description: Valise/Sholis interview; own-your-data thesis
reveals: Signing with a gallery means losing ownership of your provenance/sales record. 'Own your data, own your career' is a wedge that resonates hardest with the growing gallery-light segment.
quote: many do not own their data once they establish a relationship with a gallery they offload the cataloguing and tracking to that gallery

title: most asked question among young artists is how to price
description: Artist Substack; pricing as #1 pain
reveals: Direct third-party validation of the pricing-engine moat. The market repeatedly asks the exact question Artelier answers deterministically. Lead the funnel with pricing.
quote: the most asked question online among young artists is how to price my original art

title: art is worth what someone will pay for it
description: Pricing guide; the arbitrariness belief
reveals: The enemy is the belief that pricing is arbitrary. A deterministic engine reframes pricing from anxiety to defensible logic. Attack the arbitrariness head-on in copy.
quote: art is worth what someone will pay for it

title: be prepared to explain how you determined the value
description: Pricing guide; defensibility need
reveals: Artists want defensibility in front of buyers and galleries. Surface the why behind each price (size, tier, comp) as a collector-facing rationale, a feature manual pricing cannot match.
quote: be prepared to explain how you've determined your artwork's value

title: maintain consistent pricing across all sales channels
description: Pricing guide; consistency as trust
reveals: Consistency is a stated professional value; a single source-of-truth engine enforces it automatically. Especially valuable for managers juggling multiple artists and channels.
quote: maintain consistent pricing across all sales channels

title: not sure whether it is worth $29 per month
description: ArtPlacer review; price-to-value doubt despite lock-in
reveals: Value doubt is explicit even among users who stay (locked in, not loyal). Artelier's trial must deliver an unmissable value moment in week one or churn risk is high.
quote: not sure whether it is worth $29 per month but I will continue to use it as it is the only AR app available

title: two years and have to downgrade to 2 stars
description: ArtPlacer review; churn from stagnation
reveals: Long-term churn is driven by stagnation, promised features that never ship. A user-facing changelog/roadmap could be a retention feature, not just internal hygiene.
quote: I initially gave it 5 stars using the app for more than 2 years and have to downgrade to 2

title: canceled and they keep charging me
description: Artlogic G2 review; billing-trust failure
reveals: The leader's worst failure is billing trust plus dead support. Make cancellation frictionless and self-serve and say so: 'cancel in one click, no hostage tactics.'

title: built by developers not people who run galleries
description: Independent review aggregating 4 gallery directors
reveals: Heard near-verbatim from four directors; a systematic empathy gap. Artelier's wedge is workflow-native design; the 7-stage state machine mirrors how art actually changes hands.
content: Captured 2026-06-11. Independent review of Artlogic, ARTERNAL, ArtBinder.

title: price increases with no feature improvements PE-owned
description: Independent review; extraction narrative
reveals: PE ownership equals an extraction narrative buyers resent. Artelier's founder ownership plus 15% community fund is the anti-extraction counter-story. This is where give-back becomes positioning.
content: Captured 2026-06-11. One Berlin gallery cited ~40% increase over 18 months.

title: basic reports require exporting to Excel
description: Independent review; incumbent pushes users back to spreadsheets
reveals: Even the leader dumps users back into the spreadsheet they paid to escape. Artelier's zero-export native one-sheet is a demonstrable superiority; show a side-by-side.

title: a cost that only makes sense for established artists
description: Hands-on Artlogic review; prices out the solo artist
reveals: The leader explicitly prices out Artelier's exact ICP at £492/yr. EUR 9-39/mo is a structural price wedge, and the underserved segment is also the growing one.

title: replicate gallery bureaucracy
description: Hands-on Artlogic review; complexity as a bug for independents
reveals: Complexity is a feature for galleries and a bug for independents. A product-discipline warning: resist feature-creep toward gallery-grade complexity; guard onboarding simplicity.

title: ArtPlacer architected for galleries and full-time professionals
description: Competitor comparison; ArtPlacer is adjacent not core
reveals: ArtPlacer competes on visualization (AR), not business operations. Own the transaction backbone it ignores; treat it as a possible integration, not a rival.

title: misleading offer first it says it is free
description: ArtPlacer Shopify reviews; trial transparency complaints
reveals: Trial/pricing transparency is a sore point across competitors. Artelier's card-required trial must state day-30 behavior plainly; transparency itself is a differentiator here.
content: Captured 2026-06-11. Includes refund-refusal complaint on annual plan.

title: Artwork Archive owns most affordable and intuitive
description: AA category positioning at $6/mo
reveals: AA owns affordable+intuitive+all-in-one inventory at $6/mo. Artelier cannot out-cheap it on inventory; the durable wedge is the pricing engine plus sale workflow AA is silent on.

title: Artwork Archive entrenchment 4.9 stars 106 reviews
description: AA scale and segment dominance
reveals: AA is entrenched and loved in Artelier's exact segment. Win by category re-definition (business OS with pricing engine), not a feature war on AA's turf.

title: global art market $59.6B in 2025 up 4%
description: Art Basel UBS 2026 report; topline
reveals: TAM is large and recovering but the topline is the wrong frame for Artelier's ICP. Use for scale only; build the narrative on the segment trend.
content: Total global art sales rose 4% YoY to ~USD 59.6B in 2025.

title: lower-end strong dealers under $500k double-digit growth
description: Art Basel UBS 2026 report; segment trend
reveals: Artelier's ICP is the single growing segment while the top whipsaws. This is the core investment thesis: build rails for the expanding, ignored segment.
content: Double-digit increases in average sales for dealers turning over less than $500,000.

title: middle market $50k-250k down 29% since 2010
description: Artsy on Art Basel UBS report; the collapsing middle
reveals: The squeezed middle is hollowing out; activity moves to lower prices and more transactions. More transactions at lower values is a per-seat SaaS tailwind. Quantify in the raise.

title: 40.5M transactions up 3% as value fell
description: Art Basel UBS report; volume decoupling from value
reveals: Artelier monetizes per-artist/per-workflow, not per-dollar-of-art, so it benefits from rising transaction volume regardless of art prices. Insulated from value swings.
content: Transaction count grew 3% to 40.5 million in 2024 as value fell 12%.

title: France +9% to $4.5B Switzerland +13%
description: Art Basel UBS 2026 report; home and Legal Studio markets growing
reveals: France (home) and Switzerland (Legal Studio target) both grow above the global average. France-first launch is defensible on data, not just convenience.
content: France grew 9% to $4.5B; Switzerland up 13% in 2025.

title: online sales $10.5B 76% above 2019 social channels opening
description: Art Basel UBS report; direct/social selling elevated
reveals: Direct/social selling is structurally elevated, validating the Facebook-DM motion and the shelved WhatsApp pipeline. Instrument the DM; don't build a marketplace.
content: Online sales $10.5B in 2024, still 76% above 2019; Instagram/WeChat channels opening.
